---
title: "True modularity"
description: "Modular at the execution level, not just in the source tree."
order: 20
---

Most pipelines call themselves modular because their *source* is tidy —
functions in files, steps in folders. OpenPipeline is modular at the level that
actually matters for running an analysis: the **execution level**.

## Code-level vs. execution-level modularity

**Code-level modularity** organizes source: a well-named function, a script per
step. But those pieces are still stitched together by shared imports, a shared
environment, and glue code. Swapping one out means editing the others.

**Execution-level modularity** means each step is an *independently built,
versioned, containerized unit* that runs on its own. In OpenPipeline every
component is:

- **Self-contained** — its dependencies live in its own Docker image, so two
  components can need conflicting library versions and still run in the same
  pipeline.
- **Independently runnable** — you can execute, test, or build any component on
  its own with `viash run` / `viash test`, without the rest of the pipeline.
- **A drop-in unit** — because every component reads and writes the same
  [MuData](mudata.qmd) file through a generated CLI, one can be placed before,
  after, or in place of another with no format conversion and no glue code.

## Why it matters

Building a pipeline becomes composition, not integration. Adding a step,
removing one, or swapping an integration method is a change to the *pipeline
definition* — not a refactor of shared code. A component written for the core
[`openpipeline`](ecosystem.qmd) package works unchanged in a satellite package,
because the unit of reuse is the built component, not a snippet of source.

This is what the [engine — Viash and Viash Hub](viash.qmd) is built to deliver,
and what lets the [ecosystem](ecosystem.qmd) of packages share one set of
components.
